# Research Basis

This file documents the empirical evidence behind specific design decisions in
the cited-research skill. Each section links a skill design choice to the
research that supports it. Citations use the same format as the skill's own
output.

Contributors modifying the skill should read relevant sections before changing
the behavior they document. If new research supersedes a finding here, update
both this file and the skill instruction it supports.

---

## Source Quality and Weighting

The skill assigns quality tiers to sources (peer-reviewed > institutional >
established publications > blogs/forums) and considers source recency relative
to topic modernity.

**Why source quality tiers matter:** AI search engines consistently
misattribute and fabricate citations. A study of 1,600 queries across eight AI
search engines found >60% incorrect answers overall, with fabricated URLs and
misattributed quotes common across all platforms [1]. Source quality tiers help
prioritize which sources to trust when conflicts arise.

**Why source age is topic-dependent:** Link rot and content drift affect older
sources disproportionately — 25% of 2013-2023 web pages are already
inaccessible [2], and 72% of links from 1998 in the New York Times archive
have rotted [3]. However, for stable domains (physics, music theory,
established engineering), older sources may be the only authoritative ones.
The skill distinguishes fast-moving topics (prefer sources < 2 years) from
stable topics (older sources acceptable).

### Citations

**[1]** "We Compared Eight AI Search Engines. They're All Bad at Citing News."
*Columbia Journalism Review / Tow Center*, 2026.
<https://www.cjr.org/tow_center/we-compared-eight-ai-search-engines-theyre-all-bad-at-citing-news.php>
Data: 1,600 queries, >60% incorrect answers, Grok-3 94% incorrect, DeepSeek
misattributed 115/200, Gemini >50% fabricated URLs.

**[2]** "When Online Content Disappears." *Pew Research Center Data Labs*,
May 2024.
<https://www.pewresearch.org/data-labs/2024/05/17/when-online-content-disappears/>
Data: 25% of 2013-2023 pages inaccessible, 38% of 2013 pages gone, 54% of
Wikipedia references contain dead links.

**[3]** Perkins, J.; Bogen, M.; Hess, A. et al. "Linkrot and Content Drift in
the New York Times." *Columbia Journalism Review* / Harvard Law School, 2024.
<https://www.cjr.org/analysis/linkrot-content-drift-new-york-times.php>
Data: 2,283,445 links from 553,693 NYT articles, 25% of deep links
inaccessible, 72% of 1998 links rotted, 13% content drift in intact links.

---

## Source Inaccessibility (20-30% Planning)

The skill requires 2-3 candidate sources per key data point and expects
20-30% of sources to be inaccessible during any research session.

**Evidence for the 20-30% rate:** Pew Research found 25% of 2013-2023 content
already inaccessible [2]. Medical reference hygiene studies show decay rates
of 3.8% at 3 months, 10% at 15 months, and 38% over 10 years [4]. On top of
organic link rot, 1M+ Cloudflare customers began blocking AI crawlers in
September 2024, and Cloudflare powers ~20% of the web [5].

### Citations

**[4]** Ott, D.E. "Reference Hygiene and Death on the Internet." *PMC*, 2022.
<https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8896816/>
Data: 70% reference rot in medical articles, decay rates (3.8% at 3 months,
10% at 15 months), median lifespan 9.3 years.

**[5]** "Cloudflare just changed how AI crawlers scrape the Internet at large."
*Cloudflare Press Release*, 2025.
<https://www.cloudflare.com/press/press-releases/2025/cloudflare-just-changed-how-ai-crawlers-scrape-the-internet-at-large/>
Data: 1M+ customers blocking AI crawlers since September 2024.

---

## Source Triage as Human Gate

The skill presents inaccessible high-priority sources to the user after
iteration 2 (deep read), before proceeding to gap-fill or writing.

**Evidence:** Anthropic's research on agent autonomy found that agents
self-pause on complex tasks more than twice as frequently as humans interrupt
them [6]. Source triage is one of the highest-value human intervention points
because users often have institutional access, cached copies, or bookmarks
that resolve sources agents cannot reach.

### Citations

**[6]** "Measuring Agent Autonomy." *Anthropic Research*, 2026.
<https://www.anthropic.com/research/measuring-agent-autonomy>
Data: Agent self-pauses 2× more than human interrupts on complex tasks,
experienced users auto-approve 40%+.

---

## Citation Audit: Entailment vs. Presence

The citation audit agent grades claims using PARTIAL in addition to VERIFIED,
INACCURATE, INACCESSIBLE, and NOT FOUND. PARTIAL means the source addresses
the topic but doesn't directly support the specific claim as stated.

**Evidence:** The AAR auditability framework defines four properties for
auditable research agents [7]. Two are relevant here:
- **Provenance Coverage**: What fraction of claims have traceable source paths?
- **Provenance Soundness**: Do cited sources actually *entail* the attributed
  claims — not just resolve to real pages?

A source mentioning a related concept is not the same as a source supporting
a specific claim. The PARTIAL grade catches this distinction, which is where
subtle misrepresentation hides.

### Citations

**[7]** Rasheed, R.A.; Banerjee, S.; Mukherjee, A.; Hazra, R. "From Fluent to
Verifiable: Claim-Level Auditability for Deep Research Agents."
arXiv:2602.13855, 2026.
<https://arxiv.org/html/2602.13855v1>
Data: AAR auditability framework (Provenance Coverage, Provenance Soundness,
Contradiction Transparency, Audit Effort), goal drift 0.25-0.93 across 12 LLMs.

---

## Contradiction Transparency

The skill requires writers to surface source disagreements explicitly rather
than silently selecting one interpretation. The consistency review agent checks
for suppressed contradictions.

**Evidence:** Contradiction Transparency is one of four auditability properties
in the AAR framework [7]. When evidence conflicts are suppressed through
aggregation (e.g., averaging conflicting numbers or choosing one source without
noting the disagreement), the audit trail breaks — downstream readers cannot
assess which interpretation to trust.

---

## Accountability Clause: Structural, Not Social

The skill tells research and writing agents that independent auditors will
verify their output. This clause is kept because it reinforces the inline
citation requirement, but the skill does not claim it modifies agent behavior
through social pressure.

**Evidence:** No published study validates the behavioral effect of telling an
LLM "an auditor will verify your claims." The closest evidence is Microsoft's
"dual-error" principle: requiring inline citations forces a model to fabricate
both a false claim AND a false citation simultaneously, making fabrication
structurally harder [8]. The structural constraint (inline citations checked
against sources) is well-supported. The social pressure mechanism is plausible
but unvalidated.

### Citations

**[8]** "Prompt engineering techniques." *Microsoft Azure Foundry
Documentation*, updated March 2025.
<https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/openai/concepts/prompt-engineering>
Data: Inline citations force "two errors" (fabricated claim + fabricated
citation), grounding via supporting content.

---

## Persona Prompt Prohibition

Agent prompts in this skill use task-oriented framing ("You are researching
[DIMENSION]") and never use expert persona framing ("You are an expert in...").

**Evidence:** The PRISM study found expert persona prompts reduce factual
accuracy by 3.6 percentage points on MMLU (71.6% → 68.0%) [9]. The mechanism:
persona activation competes with factual recall for processing resources. While
PRISM tested multiple-choice accuracy rather than research synthesis, the
directional finding (personas hurt factual precision) applies — research agents
need maximum factual recall, not role-playing.

### Citations

**[9]** Hu, Z.; Rostami, M.; Thomason, J. "PRISM: Expert Personas Improve
Alignment but Damage Accuracy." University of Southern California,
arXiv:2603.18507, March 2026.
<https://arxiv.org/html/2603.18507v1>
Data: MMLU accuracy drop 71.6% → 68.0% with persona prompts, safety alignment
+17.7% with Safety Monitor persona.

---

## Model Assignment by Agent Role

The skill specifies model tiers per agent type: Sonnet for discovery/audit,
Opus for analysis.

**Evidence:** A 770M parameter model (MiniCheck) matches GPT-4 accuracy at
fact-checking when given source material, at 400× lower cost [10]. Verification
is structurally easier than generation — Jason Wei's "Verifier's Rule" [11]
establishes that verification difficulty is often asymmetrically lower than
generation difficulty. The Weaver framework achieves 87.7% accuracy by
combining Llama 3.3 70B with weak verifiers, outperforming GPT-4o at 69% [12].

This supports using lighter models for mechanical tasks (search query
generation, claim-vs-source comparison) while reserving heavier models for
tasks requiring synthesis and reasoning (deep content analysis).

### Citations

**[10]** Tang, L.; Laban, P.; Durrett, G. "MiniCheck: Efficient Fact-Checking
of LLMs on Grounding Documents." EMNLP 2024, arXiv:2404.10774.
<https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.10774>
Data: 770M parameter model matching GPT-4 accuracy at 400× lower cost.

**[11]** Wei, J. "Asymmetry of Verification and Verifier's Law."
*jasonwei.net blog*, 2025.
<https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law>
Data: Five properties of verifiable tasks, asymmetry between solving and
verification difficulty.

**[12]** Saad-Falcon, J.; Buchanan, E.K.; Chen, M.F. et al. "Shrinking the
Generation-Verification Gap with Weak Verifiers." NeurIPS 2025,
arXiv:2506.18203.
<https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.18203>
Data: Weaver framework 87.7% accuracy with Llama 3.3 70B (vs GPT-4o 69.0%).

---

## Cross-Source Synthesis Limitation

The skill flags cross-source synthesis as a known LLM weakness and recommends
operators review cross-document claims more carefully.

**Evidence:** A UK Government comparative study found AI-assisted evidence
review saved 56% on analysis (single-document extraction) but required 48%
more time on revision — the phase where cross-document synthesis and narrative
integration happen [13]. AI was described as producing analysis resembling "an
undergrad essay" in synthesis quality. The savings come from automating
labor-intensive single-document analysis, not from improving cross-document
integration.

### Citations

**[13]** "AI-Assisted vs Human-Only Evidence Review." *UK Government /
Behavioural Insights Team*, April 2025.
<https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ai-assisted-vs-human-only-evidence-review/ai-assisted-vs-human-only-evidence-review-results-from-a-comparative-study>
Data: 23% overall time reduction, 56% analysis phase savings, +48% revision
phase time, only 4/42 papers in both human and AI lists.

---

## Retracted Citation Persistence

The update workflow re-fetches every cited URL rather than trusting cached
extractions. Claims that lose all citation support are removed and logged
in a retraction ledger. The skill does not attempt to re-research a failing
citation to rescue an existing claim — that reintroduces a "this claim must
be true" bias.

**Evidence:** Studies show that 90% of retracted papers continue to be cited
after retraction, often without any note of the retraction [15, Dim 3 of
agentic-research-bias]. Trusting cached extractions during an update run
is citation laundering — it preserves claims whose evidentiary basis may
have disappeared.

**Design choice — no rescue research:** When a citation fails, the skill
removes the claim rather than searching for a replacement source to support
the same conclusion. If the claim is still accurate, the discovery agents
will independently find fresh sources for it. Searching specifically to
confirm a pre-existing claim is confirmation bias by design.

---

## Content Drift Detection (Update Workflow)

The update workflow compares re-fetched content against original extraction
descriptions to detect content drift — pages that load successfully but whose
content has materially changed.

**Evidence:** 13% of intact links in the New York Times archive showed
significant content drift — the page loads but the content changed materially
[3]. Standard link-checking (HTTP 200 = OK) misses this entirely. For
citation-backed research, a URL that resolves but no longer contains the cited
data is functionally equivalent to a broken link.

---

## Validation Plan for Anti-bias Phases

Phases 1-5 change skill behavior. This section documents the validation
criteria and test design for confirming the changes produce intended effects
without regressions.

### Fresh-research test

Run the updated skill end-to-end on a topic that invites counter-perspective
(e.g., "should we use microservices?"). Observe:

- **Multi-engine search (Phase 1):** URL pool includes results from both
  WebSearch and DuckDuckGo. Dedup removes exact-URL duplicates.
- **Counter-perspective (Phase 2):** Citation pool includes sources that
  push back on the premise. Writer surfaces dissenting views as part of
  normal synthesis, not in a separate "counter" section.
- **Reflection (Phase 3):** Writing and audit agents perform one reflection
  pass. Compare grade distribution with/without reflection if feasible.
- **Framing challenge (Phase 5):** Phase 0 output includes assumptions
  surfaced from the question.

### Update-workflow test

Run the refresh path on an existing topic >6 months old with ~30+ citations.
Observe:

- **Fresh re-fetch (Phase 4):** All cited URLs are re-fetched, not trusted
  from cache. Drift is detected and classified (OK / 404 / drift / retracted).
- **Retraction ledger:** `retraction-log.md` is written if any claims are
  removed. Entries include the removed claim text, failed citation, and reason.
- **Claim consistency:** Deliverable is internally consistent after claim
  removal — no dangling citation references, no orphan sentences.

### Measurement approach

This is a qualitative review, not a controlled experiment. Document
observations and unexpected outcomes. Do not over-claim effect sizes.
If any phase underperforms, capture tuning recommendations as a follow-up
plan — do not mix tuning into the validation run.

---

## Framing Challenge

The skill surfaces the assumptions embedded in the user's research question
during Phase 0 dimension discovery. The coordinator states what the question
implicitly concludes, what frame of reference it adopts, and what it
excludes — then presents this alongside the proposed dimensions for user
review.

**Evidence:** Expert-opinion anchoring has zero exceptions across tested
LLMs — when a question implies a conclusion, confirmation bias reduces
counter-evidence detection by up to 93.5 percentage points [16]. The
skill's dimension-proposal step runs on the user's prompt as input, making
it subject to the same anchoring effect. Surfacing assumptions explicitly
forces the user to decide whether to accept them, rather than allowing
them to silently shape the entire research pipeline.

**Accepted limitation:** The framing challenge is generated by an LLM
reading the user's prompt, so it is subject to the same anchoring biases
it aims to surface. No mitigation eliminates expert-opinion anchoring;
this is harm reduction. The design stays minimal (one to three bullet
points) to avoid producing noise the user ignores.

---

## Self-reflection Intervention

The skill adds a single reflection prompt at the end of writing, citation
audit, consistency review, and research analysis agents. The prompt asks
the agent to reconsider its output before finalizing — checking for
overclaimed evidence, dismissed alternatives, and missed discrepancies.

**Evidence:** A study of 14 LLMs found a 64.5% average self-correction
blind-spot rate — models fail to correct their own errors most of the time.
However, a simple "wait, reconsider" intervention reduced this blind-spot
rate by 89.3% [17]. The intervention is a single prompt turn, not a
chain-of-thought expansion, which preserves the "fast thinking" design
principle (see §Fast Thinking in Agent Prompts).

**Design choice — one pass:** A single reflection step balances the 89.3%
blind-spot reduction against the cost of additional tokens. Multi-pass
reflection would conflict with the Search-R1 finding [14] that longer
explicit reasoning degrades research agent performance.

### Citations

**[17]** Tsui, K. "Self-Correction Blind Spot in LLMs."
arXiv:2507.02778, 2025.
<https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.02778>
Data: 14 models, 64.5% average blind-spot rate. "Wait" intervention:
89.3% reduction. Controlled error injection at three complexity levels.

---

## Counter-perspective Discovery

The skill dispatches Counter-Discovery agents alongside Discovery agents to
seek contradicting evidence, failure cases, and minority viewpoints. Counter-
source URLs merge into the same pool as supporting sources — no tagging
distinguishes them to the writer. This prevents the writer from weighting
sources differently based on their origin.

**Evidence:** LLM-selected citations skew toward popular, agreeable sources.
Citation popularity bias amplifies already-cited works while suppressing
dissenting or less-cited evidence [15]. Expert-opinion anchoring — where the
framing of the question biases the research direction — has zero exceptions
across tested LLMs, with confirmation bias reducing detection by up to
93.5 percentage points when framing implies a conclusion [16].

**Design choice — no tagging:** Counter-sources and supporting sources go
into one pool. Tagging would let the writer weight them differently,
reintroducing bias. The writer's Phase 3 instructions already require
surfacing contradictions (Writing Rule 5), which naturally incorporates
counter-perspectives without a separate section.

**Accepted limitation:** Counter-discovery itself is performed by an LLM
reading the user's prompt, so it is subject to the same framing biases it
aims to mitigate. This is harm reduction, not elimination.

### Citations

**[16]** Lou, J. "Anchoring Bias in LLMs." National Day School, Beijing,
arXiv:2412.06593, 2024.
<https://arxiv.org/html/2412.06593v1>
Data: Expert-opinion anchoring — zero exceptions across 12 questions.
Average anchoring index 0.45 for GPT-4 (vs 0.61 for humans). No simple
mitigation strategies effective for expert anchoring.

---

## Multi-Engine Search Diversity

The skill augments WebSearch with additional search engines (starting with
DuckDuckGo) to reduce single-engine bias in the citation pool. The
coordinator runs `scripts/multi-search.py` between discovery and deep-read
iterations, merges results, and deduplicates by URL before fetching.

**Evidence:** A study of search engine result overlap across four major
engines found that 84.9% of results are unique to a single engine, 11.4%
are shared by two engines, 2.6% by three, and only 1.1% by all four [15].
Google-Bing overlap was under 32%. Relying on a single search engine is
a structural bias that limits what sources the research can discover.

**Design choice — coordinator-invoked:** The coordinator runs the
multi-search script rather than sub-agents. This preserves the existing
security boundary where the user sees every outbound action from the main
thread. Sub-agents continue to use WebSearch for their own discovery; the
script's results are merged at the coordinator level.

### Citations

**[15]** "Comparison of Source Distribution and Result Overlap in Web
Search." arXiv:2207.07330, 2022.
<https://arxiv.org/pdf/2207.07330>
Data: 84.9% of results unique to one engine, 11.4% shared by two, 2.6%
by three, 1.1% by all four. Google-Bing overlap under 32%.

---

## Fast Thinking in Agent Prompts

Agent prompts are kept lean and task-oriented, avoiding instructions that
encourage explicit reasoning chains before taking search actions.

**Evidence:** The Search-R1 study found that longer explicit reasoning
*degrades* performance in deep research agents [14]. "Fast thinking" (direct
search/answer decisions) outperformed "slow thinking" (explicit reasoning
before each action) by 3.9-4.2% accuracy. The mechanism: tokens allocated to
reasoning reduce capacity available for search and retrieval.

### Citations

**[14]** Xu, Y.; Lu, S.; Cheng, J. et al. "How to Train Your Deep Research
Agent?" (Search-R1). CASIA/Meituan, arXiv:2602.19526, 2026.
<https://arxiv.org/html/2602.19526v1>
Data: Fast thinking template outperforms slow thinking by 3.9-4.2% accuracy.
